title: SQL Analysis & Reporting
description: A practical single-table reporting course with distinct Try It tasks, five cumulative module projects, and one integrated executive-report capstone.

modules.title: Readable Report Foundations
modules.description: Shape clear row-level reports with aliases, unique values, calculated columns, and consistent rounding.
modules.purpose: Move from basic retrieval to reports another person can read and trust.
learningObjectives: Rename result columns without changing the database schema.
Use DISTINCT to inspect available reporting categories.
Build row-level calculations from existing numeric columns.
Round presentation values while preserving the source data.
guidedExercises: Create manager-friendly headings for operational fields.
Inspect unique statuses and region-category combinations.
Calculate gross and service-adjusted order values.
Build a completed-order detail report in five cumulative steps.
modules.moduleProject: Build a completed-order value report with readable headings, rounded gross sales, and stable sorting.

sections.title: Readable Report Output
sections.description: Turn database-oriented columns into clear report output.

topics.title: Aliases for Readable Headings
topics.summary: Rename result columns so a report communicates business meaning without changing the stored table.
learningGoals: Use AS to assign a temporary result heading.
Choose concise aliases that describe the displayed value.
Explain that an alias changes query output, not the database column.

topics.title: Distinct Values for Report Categories
topics.summary: Inspect the unique values and combinations available before defining a report filter or grouping rule.
learningGoals: Return one copy of each value from a selected column.
Explain how DISTINCT applies to the complete selected row.
Use ordering to make a distinct-value audit easy to scan.

sections.title: Calculated Report Columns
sections.description: Create and format useful row-level metrics.

topics.title: Arithmetic Expressions in SELECT
topics.summary: Create row-level metrics from numeric columns while keeping the original source data unchanged.
learningGoals: Multiply quantity and unit price to calculate a row value.
Use parentheses to make a multi-part calculation explicit.
Label each calculated expression with a meaningful alias.

topics.title: Rounding Report Values
topics.summary: Format calculated numbers for a readable report without changing the precision stored in the source table.
learningGoals: Use ROUND(expression, digits) on a selected value.
Choose a precision that matches the report requirement.
Explain why rounding output is different from changing stored data.

sections.title: Module Project
sections.description: Build one clean detail report without discarding earlier work.

topics.title: Module 0 Project: Completed Order Value Report
topics.summary: Build one readable completed-order detail report, adding aliases, a gross-sales calculation, rounding, and stable sorting without discarding earlier columns.
learningGoals: Preserve the same completed-order row set through every step.
Add one focused output improvement at a time.
Finish with exact aliases, rounded gross sales, and deterministic order.

projectBrief.scenario: A sales manager needs a clean list of completed orders for a weekly review. The raw table contains the right data, but the headings and monetary output are not presentation-ready.
projectBrief.workspace: Browser SQL editor, schema browser, and deterministic results table
projectBrief.deliverable: A single query that shows completed orders with readable headings, rounded gross sales, and highest-value orders first.
requirements: Keep one valid SQL SELECT statement in every step.
Use the previous full solution as the next starter query.
Preserve every earlier requirement unless the current step explicitly replaces it.
Run against sales_reporting and verify non-empty, deterministic output.
Do not introduce joins, subqueries, CTEs, window functions, or mutation SQL.

stepLadder.title: Select the Completed Order Details
stepLadder.requirement: Start the report with `order_id`, `order_date`, `product_name`, `quantity`, and `unit_price`. Include only completed orders.

stepLadder.title: Add Reader-Friendly Aliases
stepLadder.requirement: Keep the completed-order query and rename the columns `order_number`, `ordered_on`, `product`, `units`, and `unit_price`.

stepLadder.title: Add Gross Sales
stepLadder.requirement: Keep every existing column and add `quantity * unit_price` as `gross_sales`.

stepLadder.title: Round the Monetary Result
stepLadder.requirement: Keep the complete report and display `gross_sales` to two decimal places.

stepLadder.title: Rank the Report Predictably
stepLadder.requirement: Finish the report by sorting `gross_sales` from highest to lowest. Use `order_number` ascending to break ties.

modules.title: Null-Safe Calculations and Business Labels
modules.description: Control how missing values affect calculations, then translate row-level results into useful review labels.
modules.purpose: Make reporting rules explicit whenever source data may be missing.
learningObjectives: Predict how NULL changes arithmetic results.
Use COALESCE only when a documented fallback is appropriate.
Calculate gross, discount, and net values consistently.
Use CASE to label rows without hiding missing data.
guidedExercises: Observe NULL propagation in discount and rating calculations.
Replace missing discounts with a documented zero-percent fallback.
Build percentage-based monetary calculations.
Create a six-step discount audit report with review labels.
modules.moduleProject: Build a discount audit that retains raw values, calculates gross and net sales, and flags records needing review.

sections.title: Null-Safe Calculations
sections.description: Understand missing values before deciding how a report should treat them.

topics.title: How NULL Affects Calculations
topics.summary: Recognize when a missing source value makes a calculated result unknown instead of zero.
learningGoals: Predict the result of arithmetic that includes NULL.
Distinguish a missing value from a numeric zero.
Inspect missing source values before choosing a fallback rule.

topics.title: COALESCE for Missing Values
topics.summary: Apply an explicit fallback when the reporting rule defines how a missing value should be treated.
learningGoals: Return the first non-NULL value with COALESCE.
Use a zero-percent fallback only when the business rule allows it.
Keep raw and fallback values visible when an audit requires both.

sections.title: Business Calculations and Labels
sections.description: Turn percentage rules into readable financial output.

topics.title: Percentage and Discount Calculations
topics.summary: Translate a percentage stored in the table into gross, discount, and net monetary values.
learningGoals: Convert a stored percentage to a decimal rate.
Calculate discount amount from gross sales.
Calculate net sales without losing the null-safe fallback rule.

topics.title: CASE for Readable Labels
topics.summary: Turn row-level values into clear categories while handling missing data before numeric thresholds.
learningGoals: Write a searched CASE expression with ordered conditions.
Handle NULL explicitly before numeric comparisons.
Choose labels that communicate an action or interpretation.

sections.title: Module Project
sections.description: Create a review-ready discount audit in cumulative steps.

topics.title: Module 1 Project: Discount Audit Report
topics.summary: Build a row-level audit that preserves raw discount data, applies an explicit fallback for calculations, and identifies records requiring review.
learningGoals: Keep the raw discount percentage visible beside the applied value.
Preserve gross, discount, and net amounts as the query grows.
Finish with an audit label and a deterministic review order.

projectBrief.scenario: Finance needs to understand which orders have missing or unusually large discounts before using net sales in a management report.
projectBrief.workspace: Browser SQL editor, schema browser, and deterministic results table
projectBrief.deliverable: A single audit query containing raw and applied discount values, rounded financial calculations, a review label, and a priority order.
requirements: Keep one valid SQL SELECT statement in every step.
Use the previous full solution as the next starter query.
Preserve every earlier requirement unless the current step explicitly replaces it.
Run against sales_reporting and verify non-empty, deterministic output.
Do not introduce joins, subqueries, CTEs, window functions, or mutation SQL.

stepLadder.title: Create the Audit Base
stepLadder.requirement: Start with `order_id`, `product_name`, `order_status`, and the raw `discount_pct` for every order.

stepLadder.title: Add the Applied Discount Rule
stepLadder.requirement: Keep the raw fields and add `COALESCE(discount_pct, 0)` as `applied_discount_pct`.

stepLadder.title: Calculate Gross Sales
stepLadder.requirement: Keep all existing audit fields and add gross sales rounded to two decimals as `gross_sales`.

stepLadder.title: Calculate Discount Amount
stepLadder.requirement: Preserve the report and add the null-safe discount dollars rounded to two decimals as `discount_amount`.

stepLadder.title: Calculate Net Sales
stepLadder.requirement: Keep gross sales and discount amount, then add null-safe net sales rounded to two decimals as `net_sales`.

stepLadder.title: Add Review Labels and Priority
stepLadder.requirement: Finish the audit with `discount_review`: `Missing discount` for NULL, `Review high discount` for 15 percent or more, otherwise `OK`. Sort missing discounts first, then high discounts, then remaining orders; use `order_id` as the final tie-breaker.

modules.title: Aggregate Functions and KPI Snapshots
modules.description: Condense many rows into trustworthy counts, totals, averages, minimums, and maximums.
modules.purpose: Teach learners to define one-row KPIs before introducing grouped scorecards.
learningObjectives: Choose between COUNT(*) and COUNT(column).
Sum stored columns and calculated expressions.
Explain how AVG handles missing values.
Use MIN and MAX to describe a metric range.
guidedExercises: Compare total rows with non-null rating counts.
Calculate completed-order units and sales totals.
Compare rated-only averages with an explicit fallback average.
Build one cumulative completed-sales KPI row.
modules.moduleProject: Build a one-row completed-sales snapshot that preserves every metric added in earlier steps.

sections.title: Counts and Totals
sections.description: Measure activity volume and monetary value.

topics.title: Counting Rows and Values
topics.summary: Use COUNT deliberately to distinguish total records from records with a known value.
learningGoals: Use COUNT(*) to count rows.
Use COUNT(column) to count non-NULL values in one column.
Name the counted population clearly in the result.

topics.title: Summing Business Metrics
topics.summary: Add stored values or calculated row expressions across the full query scope.
learningGoals: Use SUM on a numeric column.
Use SUM on a row-level expression.
Keep the filter and metric definition aligned.

sections.title: Averages and Extremes
sections.description: Describe typical values and useful boundaries.

topics.title: Averages and NULL Behavior
topics.summary: Calculate an average and state clearly whether missing values are excluded or replaced.
learningGoals: Use AVG on a numeric column or expression.
Explain that AVG ignores NULL inputs.
Compare a rated-only average with an explicit fallback average.

topics.title: Minimums and Maximums
topics.summary: Use MIN and MAX to describe the lower and upper boundaries of a numeric metric.
learningGoals: Return the smallest and largest stored values.
Apply MIN and MAX to a calculated row expression.
Label each boundary with the metric it describes.

sections.title: Module Project
sections.description: Combine completed-order KPIs into one reliable snapshot.

topics.title: Module 2 Project: Completed Sales KPI Snapshot
topics.summary: Build one cumulative summary row for completed orders, preserving every previously added KPI.
learningGoals: Keep the report at one-row grain with no GROUP BY.
Use one documented completed-order population for every metric.
Finish with volume, value, average, rating, and range KPIs in one row.

projectBrief.scenario: The store director needs one compact snapshot of completed sales before reviewing any regional breakdowns.
projectBrief.workspace: Browser SQL editor, schema browser, and deterministic results table
projectBrief.deliverable: A one-row completed-sales KPI query with exact aliases and consistent null-safe financial definitions.
requirements: Keep one valid SQL SELECT statement in every step.
Use the previous full solution as the next starter query.
Preserve every earlier requirement unless the current step explicitly replaces it.
Run against sales_reporting and verify non-empty, deterministic output.
Do not introduce joins, subqueries, CTEs, window functions, or mutation SQL.

stepLadder.title: Count Completed Orders
stepLadder.requirement: Start the snapshot with the number of completed orders as `completed_orders`.

stepLadder.title: Add Completed Units
stepLadder.requirement: Keep `completed_orders` and add `SUM(quantity)` as `completed_units`.

stepLadder.title: Add Gross Sales
stepLadder.requirement: Preserve the volume KPIs and add completed gross sales rounded to two decimals as `gross_sales`.

stepLadder.title: Add Discount and Net Sales
stepLadder.requirement: Keep the existing KPIs and add null-safe `discount_amount` and `net_sales`, each rounded to two decimals.

stepLadder.title: Add Averages
stepLadder.requirement: Preserve every total and add `average_net_order_value` and `average_rating`, both rounded to two decimals.

stepLadder.title: Add the Net-Order Range
stepLadder.requirement: Finish the snapshot with the smallest and largest completed net order values as `smallest_net_order` and `largest_net_order`, rounded to two decimals.

modules.title: Grouped Summaries and Ranked Scorecards
modules.description: Create one summary row per group, preserve the intended report grain, and rank results predictably.
modules.purpose: Move from one overall KPI row to comparable regional and category scorecards.
learningObjectives: Group an aggregate report by one dimension.
Define the grain created by multiple grouping columns.
Sort aggregate results with deterministic tie-breakers.
Remove columns that accidentally split a report into extra groups.
guidedExercises: Summarize units and completed orders by a single business dimension.
Build region-category and representative-status summaries.
Rank grouped net sales with stable secondary sorting.
Build one cumulative region-category performance scorecard.
modules.moduleProject: Build a completed-order region-category scorecard with counts, sales KPIs, ratings, and deterministic ranking.

sections.title: Grouping Basics
sections.description: Define what one row represents in a grouped report.

topics.title: GROUP BY One Column
topics.summary: Return one aggregate summary row for each value of a chosen business dimension.
learningGoals: Group an aggregate query by one selected dimension.
State what one result row represents.
Keep every non-aggregate selected column in GROUP BY.

topics.title: GROUP BY Multiple Columns
topics.summary: Define a more detailed report grain by grouping on a deliberate combination of dimensions.
learningGoals: Group by two dimensions in the same left-to-right order used in SELECT.
Describe one result row using both grouping columns.
Recognize when an extra grouping column makes a report too detailed.

sections.title: Ranking and Grouping Quality
sections.description: Order grouped results and prevent accidental grain changes.

topics.title: Sorting Aggregate Results
topics.summary: Rank grouped metrics and add deterministic tie-breakers so repeated runs produce a predictable order.
learningGoals: Sort by an aggregate alias.
Choose ascending or descending order based on the business question.
Add dimension tie-breakers after the primary metric.

topics.title: Avoiding Grouping Mistakes
topics.summary: Protect report grain by removing detail columns that split or confuse grouped results.
learningGoals: Identify a selected detail column that does not belong in a grouped report.
Remove unnecessary grouping dimensions.
Check that each output row matches the stated report grain.

sections.title: Module Project
sections.description: Build one region-category scorecard from a stable base query.

topics.title: Module 3 Project: Region-Category Scorecard
topics.summary: Build one completed-order scorecard at region-category grain and retain every metric as the report grows.
learningGoals: Keep one row per region-category combination.
Use the same completed-order population for all KPIs.
Finish with deterministic ranking by net sales.

projectBrief.scenario: Regional leaders need a comparable category scorecard without exposing individual order rows.
projectBrief.workspace: Browser SQL editor, schema browser, and deterministic results table
projectBrief.deliverable: A grouped completed-order scorecard with volume, financial, average, and rating metrics in stable rank order.
requirements: Keep one valid SQL SELECT statement in every step.
Use the previous full solution as the next starter query.
Preserve every earlier requirement unless the current step explicitly replaces it.
Run against sales_reporting and verify non-empty, deterministic output.
Do not introduce joins, subqueries, CTEs, window functions, or mutation SQL.

stepLadder.title: Establish the Scorecard Grain
stepLadder.requirement: Return `region`, `category`, and completed-order count as `completed_orders`.

stepLadder.title: Add Completed Units
stepLadder.requirement: Keep the group key and order count, then add `SUM(quantity)` as `completed_units`.

stepLadder.title: Add Gross Sales
stepLadder.requirement: Preserve the volume KPIs and add rounded completed gross sales as `gross_sales`.

stepLadder.title: Add Discount and Net Sales
stepLadder.requirement: Keep every existing KPI and add rounded `discount_amount` and `net_sales` using the zero-percent fallback for missing discounts.

stepLadder.title: Add Average Order Value and Rating
stepLadder.requirement: Preserve all totals and add rounded `average_net_order_value` and `average_rating` for each group.

stepLadder.title: Rank the Completed Sales Scorecard
stepLadder.requirement: Finish the scorecard by sorting `net_sales` highest first, then `region` and `category` alphabetically.

modules.title: HAVING and Conditional Reporting
modules.description: Filter source rows and grouped results at the correct stage, then calculate conditional KPIs and exception labels.
modules.purpose: Turn full scorecards into focused reports that support management action.
learningObjectives: Use WHERE to control which rows enter an aggregate.
Use HAVING to keep or remove completed groups.
Calculate conditional counts and sums with CASE inside aggregates.
Assign performance labels from aggregate results.
guidedExercises: Compare row filtering with group filtering.
Use realistic HAVING thresholds that return meaningful results.
Calculate completion counts and pending value in grouped reports.
Build a seven-step exception report for region-category groups.
modules.moduleProject: Build a region-category exception report that identifies completion and rating risks without losing prior metrics.

sections.title: Filtering Before and After Grouping
sections.description: Place conditions where they match the report logic.

topics.title: WHERE Before Grouping
topics.summary: Filter source rows before aggregation so every metric describes the intended population.
learningGoals: Place row-level conditions in WHERE.
Explain how WHERE changes the rows entering each group.
Keep the metric alias aligned with the filtered population.

topics.title: HAVING After Grouping
topics.summary: Keep only completed groups whose aggregate results meet a meaningful threshold.
learningGoals: Use HAVING for aggregate conditions.
Choose thresholds that match the seeded data and business question.
Explain the difference between filtering rows and filtering groups.

sections.title: Conditional KPIs and Labels
sections.description: Calculate several outcomes from the same grouped rows.

topics.title: CASE Inside Aggregate Functions
topics.summary: Calculate condition-specific counts and monetary totals without removing the other rows from each group.
learningGoals: Count matching rows with SUM(CASE ... THEN 1 ELSE 0 END).
Sum a monetary expression only when a condition matches.
Explain why conditional aggregation differs from WHERE filtering.

topics.title: Performance Bands from Aggregates
topics.summary: Use CASE on aggregate expressions to turn grouped metrics into concise management labels.
learningGoals: Write CASE conditions that reference aggregate expressions.
Order high, middle, and lower thresholds correctly.
Use thresholds that produce meaningful groups in the seeded dataset.

sections.title: Module Project
sections.description: Build a focused exception report with calibrated rules.

topics.title: Module 4 Project: Performance Exception Report
topics.summary: Build one region-category exception report that compares total and completed activity, calculates risk metrics, and keeps only groups needing attention.
learningGoals: Preserve the full region-category population for conditional KPIs.
Use calibrated completion and rating rules that return meaningful rows.
Finish with an exception label, HAVING filter, and priority order.

projectBrief.scenario: Operations leaders want a short review queue rather than another full scorecard. Groups should appear when completion is below 75 percent or the recorded average rating is below 4.3.
projectBrief.workspace: Browser SQL editor, schema browser, and deterministic results table
projectBrief.deliverable: A grouped exception report with total orders, completed orders, completion rate, completed net sales, rating, missing-discount exposure, exception labels, and stable priority sorting.
requirements: Keep one valid SQL SELECT statement in every step.
Use the previous full solution as the next starter query.
Preserve every earlier requirement unless the current step explicitly replaces it.
Run against sales_reporting and verify non-empty, deterministic output.
Do not introduce joins, subqueries, CTEs, window functions, or mutation SQL.

stepLadder.title: Establish Total Orders by Group
stepLadder.requirement: Return `region`, `category`, and `COUNT(*)` as `total_orders` for every region-category group.

stepLadder.title: Add Completed Orders
stepLadder.requirement: Keep `total_orders` and add a conditional completed count as `completed_orders`.

stepLadder.title: Calculate Completion Rate
stepLadder.requirement: Preserve both counts and add `completion_rate` as completed orders divided by total orders, expressed as a percentage rounded to one decimal.

stepLadder.title: Add Completed Net Sales
stepLadder.requirement: Keep the completion KPIs and add completed-order net sales rounded to two decimals as `completed_net_sales` using CASE inside SUM.

stepLadder.title: Add Rating and Missing-Discount Exposure
stepLadder.requirement: Preserve the report and add `average_rating` rounded to two decimals plus `missing_discount_orders` as a conditional count.

stepLadder.title: Assign the Exception Reason
stepLadder.requirement: Keep every KPI and add `exception_reason`: `Completion and rating risk` when completion is below 75 and average rating is below 4.3, `Completion risk` when only completion is below 75, otherwise `Rating risk`.

stepLadder.title: Keep and Prioritize Only Exceptions
stepLadder.requirement: Finish the report by keeping groups with at least two orders where completion is below 75 percent or average rating is below 4.3. Sort combined risks first, completion risks second, rating risks third; within each priority sort completed net sales ascending, then region and category.

modules.title: Final Capstone: Executive Sales Performance Report
modules.description: Plan, audit, build, validate, and interpret one complete executive report using the full single-table toolkit.
modules.purpose: Integrate the course into a portfolio-quality report with a clear audience, grain, metric contract, and priority order.
learningObjectives: Translate a stakeholder brief into a precise report contract.
Audit source coverage and missing values before implementation.
Prototype grain and deterministic ordering.
Build one cumulative eight-step executive scorecard.
guidedExercises: Separate dimensions, metrics, filters, labels, and sorting rules.
Map stakeholder language to SQL expressions.
Audit status, date, and missing-value coverage.
Deliver and interpret a region-category executive report.
modules.moduleProject: Complete one eight-step executive sales performance report; the four preparation topics remain ordinary lessons.

sections.title: Capstone Planning
sections.description: Turn a business request into a precise SQL report contract.

topics.title: Reading the Capstone Reporting Brief
topics.summary: Convert a stakeholder request into dimensions, metrics, filters, labels, and ordering rules before writing SQL.
learningGoals: Identify the intended audience and decision.
State the report grain in one sentence.
Separate required dimensions, metrics, filters, and sorting rules.

topics.title: Building a Metric Map
topics.summary: Map each requested output column to its source fields, SQL expression, missing-value rule, and display format.
learningGoals: Match each stakeholder metric to a SQL expression.
Record the NULL rule and rounding rule for every calculated KPI.
Identify which metrics require conditional aggregation.

sections.title: Capstone Data Readiness
sections.description: Audit the table and verify the planned grouping before the final build.

topics.title: Auditing the Sales Reporting Table
topics.summary: Check status coverage, date coverage, and missing-value exposure before building the capstone metrics.
learningGoals: Summarize source-row coverage by status.
Count missing discounts and ratings in one audit row.
Verify the minimum and maximum order dates.

topics.title: Validating Report Grain and Order
topics.summary: Prototype the final group key and stable ranking before adding every capstone metric.
learningGoals: Confirm that the prototype returns one row per region-category combination.
Use a small aggregate to test the planned grain.
Add deterministic sorting before the final query becomes complex.

sections.title: Final Capstone Project
sections.description: Build the final report through eight cumulative query snapshots.

topics.title: Final Capstone: Executive Sales Performance Report
topics.summary: Build one January 2026 region-category report that combines volume, completion, sales, ratings, data quality, filtering, labels, and deterministic ranking.
learningGoals: Keep the January region-category grain unchanged through all eight steps.
Preserve every prior KPI while adding the next requirement.
Finish with calibrated performance bands and a meaningful HAVING rule.

projectBrief.scenario: Leadership needs one concise report for the January performance review. It should reveal which region-category groups combine meaningful completed sales with reliable execution, while still showing missing-discount and rating context.
projectBrief.role: Business intelligence analyst presenting to leadership
projectBrief.workspace: Browser SQL editor, schema browser, and deterministic results table
projectBrief.deliverable: One eight-step cumulative SQL report with a validated final query and an output that supports evidence-based discussion.
requirements: Keep one valid SQL SELECT statement in every step.
Use the previous full solution as the next starter query.
Preserve every earlier requirement unless the current step explicitly replaces it.
Run against sales_reporting and verify non-empty, deterministic output.
Do not introduce joins, subqueries, CTEs, window functions, or mutation SQL.

stepLadder.title: Set the Period and Report Grain
stepLadder.requirement: Start with January 2026. Return `region`, `category`, and `COUNT(*)` as `total_orders`, with one row per region-category combination.

stepLadder.title: Add Completed Orders
stepLadder.requirement: Preserve the period, grain, and total count. Add a conditional completed-order count as `completed_orders`.

stepLadder.title: Add Completion Rate
stepLadder.requirement: Keep both counts and add `completion_rate` as a percentage rounded to one decimal.

stepLadder.title: Add Gross Sales
stepLadder.requirement: Preserve the completion KPIs and add all-order gross sales rounded to two decimals as `gross_sales`.

stepLadder.title: Add Completed Net Sales
stepLadder.requirement: Keep every prior KPI and add null-safe completed-order net sales rounded to two decimals as `completed_net_sales`.

stepLadder.title: Add Rating and Discount-Data Context
stepLadder.requirement: Preserve every existing output and add `average_rating` rounded to two decimals plus `missing_discount_orders`.

stepLadder.title: Assign a Performance Band
stepLadder.requirement: Keep the complete scorecard and add `performance_band`: `Strong` when completed net sales are at least 150 and completion rate is at least 75; `Stable` when completed net sales are at least 100 and completion rate is at least 50; otherwise `Watch`.

stepLadder.title: Filter and Rank the Executive Report
stepLadder.requirement: Finish the capstone by keeping groups with at least two completed orders or at least 150 in completed net sales. Sort by `completed_net_sales` descending, then `region` and `category` ascending.
